records.ety: [OE. canker, cancre, AS. cancer (akin to D. kanker, OHG chanchar.), fr. L. cancer a cancer; or if a native word, cf. Gr. <?/ excrescence on tree, <?/ gangrene. Cf. also OF. cancre, F. chancere, fr. L. cancer. See cancer, and cf. Chancre.]

defs: A corroding or sloughing ulcer; esp. a spreading gangrenous ulcer or collection of ulcers in or about the mouth; -- called also water canker, canker of the mouth, and noma.
Anything which corrodes, corrupts, or destroy.
quotes: The cankers of envy and faction.

defs: A disease incident to trees, causing the bark to rot and fall off.
An obstinate and often incurable disease of a horse's foot, characterized by separation of the horny portion and the development of fungoid growths; -- usually resulting from neglected thrush.
A kind of wild, worthless rose; the dog-rose.
quotes: To put down Richard, that sweet lovely rose. And plant this thorm, this canker, Bolingbroke.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Cankered (-krd); p. pr. & vb. n. Cankering.]

defs: To affect as a canker; to eat away; to corrode; to consume.
quotes: No lapse of moons can canker Love.

defs: To infect or pollute; to corrupt.
quotes: A tithe purloined cankers the whole estate.

defs: To waste away, grow rusty, or be oxidized, as a mineral.
quotes: Silvering will sully and canker more than gliding.

defs: To be or become diseased, or as if diseased, with canker; to grow corrupt; to become venomous.
quotes: As with age his body uglier grows, So his mind cankers.
